candidate_problems.text: Can the vague notion of 'equilibrium lock-in avoidance' be made into a precise, lawlike criterion on cosmological states and dynamics that is both necessary and nontrivially sufficient for open-ended structure formation? The key unresolved tension is whether the conjecture is genuinely explanatory or just a redescription of low-entropy initial conditions in new language. A worthwhile problem is to formalize anti-lock-in in terms of gravitational entropy, free-energy gradients, metastability, and persistence of non-equilibrium subsystems, then test whether this criterion picks out a narrower and more explanatory class of universes than standard fine-tuning talk.

What fundamental mechanism, if any, selects or generates the required anti-lock-in initial condition without reintroducing teleology, ad hoc boundary conditions, or hidden anthropic reasoning? The conjecture shifts the question from 'fine-tuned for us' to 'not born in dead equilibrium,' but it remains open whether any known cosmological framework actually explains that asymmetry rather than assuming it. This is a major open problem because the conjecture's value depends on whether inflation, bounce models, cosmological natural selection, measure proposals, or new lawlike boundary principles can produce such states generically.

Is the link from anti-lock-in conditions to knowledge-bearing complexity actually robust across nearby possible universes, or does it smuggle in additional fine-tuning from microphysics, chemistry, and timescale structure? The unresolved tension is between claiming that knowledge growth is a generic consequence of avoiding equilibrium and the possibility that many anti-lock-in universes still fail to produce stable stars, rich chemistry, error correction, or long horizons for cumulative evolution. Exploring this would clarify whether anti-lock-in is the main bottleneck or only one necessary condition among many equally special constraints.
